pattern: Only dry a lake the moment it is about to flood again, always picking the earliest still-unused dry day after it last filled - a Fenwick tree turns 'find the earliest free day past this point' into a fast lookup instead of a scan.
hints: A flood only happens when the same lake fills twice with no dry day for it in between, so track the day each lake last filled and only worry about a lake once it rains on it a second time.
When a lake is about to re-flood, you need the earliest dry day that is still unused and comes after the lake's previous fill - using it any later only removes flexibility for other lakes.

Keep the set of unused dry-day indices in a structure that supports 'smallest unused index greater than X' and removal in better than linear time (a Fenwick/binary-indexed tree over day indices works well) so the whole pass stays fast even at the maximum input size.

edge_inputs.description: no lake ever repeats, so no drying is ever required
a lake refills after being dried in between, avoiding the flood
it never rains at all, so every day is a free choice
a single day with nothing to dry
a single day of rain with no repeats possible
two lakes are full but only one truly needs drying before its next rain - drying the wrong one must fail validation
the same lake floods on back-to-back days with no chance to dry it in between - impossible
a dry day exists but only before the lake fills the first time, so it cannot prevent the later repeat - impossible

stress.description: near-max length with a modest lake pool and plenty of dry days
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    rains = [rng.choice([0] * 3 + list(range(1, 200))) for _ in range(n)]
    return [rains]

stress.description: near-max length with very few dry days, likely to be infeasible
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    rains = [rng.choice([0] + [1, 2, 3] * 40) for _ in range(n)]
    return [rains]
